whatItDoes: Analyses password strength in real-time as you type, providing entropy calculations, estimated crack time, character pool analysis, and actionable feedback tips. Displays a visual strength meter for intuitive assessment.
whyItExists: Understanding password strength goes beyond simple 'weak/medium/strong' labels. This tool provides the underlying entropy mathematics and practical crack-time estimates so developers and users can make informed decisions about password security.
whoShouldUse: Developers designing password policies, security-conscious users evaluating their passwords, and anyone building registration or login systems.
useCases: Evaluate password strength during account creation to enforce security policies
Understand the entropy and crack resistance of existing passwords
Test password generators by analysing the output strength
Educate users on why certain passwords are stronger than others
Determine appropriate password length requirements for an application
instructions: Type or paste a password in the input field — analysis updates in real time
Observe the visual strength meter indicating overall strength level
Review the entropy calculation showing the bits of entropy (higher is better)
Check the character pool analysis showing counts of uppercase, lowercase, digits, and symbols
Read the feedback tips for suggestions to improve the password
examples: Password: 'password123' → Low entropy, fast crack time, feedback: add symbols and uppercase
Password: 'Tr0ub4dor&3' → Moderate entropy, moderate crack time, feedback: consider longer length
Password: 'correcthorsebatterystaple' → High entropy due to length, despite lower character diversity
bestPractices: Aim for at least 80 bits of entropy for passwords protecting sensitive accounts
Passphrase-based passwords (long, memorable words) often provide more entropy than short complex passwords
A password of 16+ characters with mixed character types is considered very strong
Use password managers to generate and store truly random passwords
commonMistakes: Relying only on character complexity while keeping passwords short
Assuming a password is strong because it contains one uppercase letter and one number
Using common dictionary words or predictable patterns like 'Password1!'
Ignoring the length factor — each additional character dramatically increases crack time
faq: How is entropy calculated? Using the formula log2(charset_size^length), which estimates the number of possible combinations.
What does 'bits of entropy' mean? Each bit doubles the number of possible combinations; 80+ bits is considered strong.
Why is my long passphrase rated highly? Length is the most important factor — long passwords have exponentially more combinations.
Is the crack time estimate realistic? It's a lower bound assuming offline brute-force; real-world attacks may be faster or slower.
